planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Opalka is a Polish heirloom paste tomato renowned for producing extraordinarily long, pointed, banana-pepper-shaped fruits 4–8 inches in length with extremely thick, meaty walls, minimal seeds, concentrated flavor, and very low moisture content — considered by many tomato growers the finest heirloom paste variety for sauce and preserving.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors after frost into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; provide staking or caging as plants grow 5–7 feet tall. Opalka is a Polish heirloom paste tomato (named for the Opalka family who brought it to America) producing distinctively long, tapering, banana-pepper to Italian horn-shaped fruits of 4–8 inches with a pointed blossom end and the distinctive characteristic of exceedingly thick, dense walls surrounding very small seed cavities — the result is a paste tomato with more dry matter per pound than virtually any other variety, producing sauce with exceptional body and concentrated tomato flavor without hours of reduction time. The flavor fresh is rich and complex, with more sweetness and less tartness than most red paste types; the thick walls and dry flesh make Opalka exceptionally well-suited to drying (dried Opalka are comparable to sun-dried tomatoes in Italy). An indeterminate plant that bears a large late-season crop; very productive in Southern and Gulf Coast gardens in spring and fall seasons.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Stupice is a Czech heirloom from Stupice in the former Czechoslovakia, one of the earliest-ripening tomatoes available for cool-climate and short-season gardens — producing ripe fruit in 52–65 days from transplant with exceptional cold tolerance that allows fruit set at temperatures too low for most varieties.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors after danger of frost passes; Stupice is unusually tolerant of cold nighttime temperatures and can be transplanted somewhat earlier than standard heat-loving varieties. Stupice (pronounced 'STOO-peet-seh') originates from the village of Stupice in what is now the Czech Republic and was brought to the Seed Savers Exchange network by gardener Dr. Carolyn Male in the early 1990s, where it rapidly became one of the most popular heirloom tomatoes in cold-climate gardens of the Pacific Northwest, Canada, and Northern states. It is a potato-leaved, indeterminate variety producing clusters of small to medium (2–4 oz), round, uniformly deep red fruits — not large by beefsteak standards, but remarkable for their combination of earliness (ripening weeks before most other varieties) and rich, full-flavored, sweet-tart tomato character that is often absent in other very-early varieties. The ability to set fruit in cool temperatures makes Stupice the first choice for short-season gardeners in coastal climates and high altitudes; it also makes an excellent spring variety for Gulf Coast and Southern gardens planted early in the season.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Azoychka is a rare Russian heirloom yellow-orange tomato of uncertain origin brought to the Seed Savers Exchange from Moscow in the early 1990s, producing medium-sized, flattened, lemon-yellow to pale orange fruits with an exceptionally bright, tangy, citrus-touched flavor unlike most yellow heirloom tomatoes, which tend toward the sweet and bland.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost into fertile, well-drained, deeply prepared soil; provide staking or caging as plants grow 4–6 feet tall. Azoychka is a Russian heirloom yellow tomato brought to American seed preservation networks from Moscow in the early 1990s and distributed through the Seed Savers Exchange; its exact origins are unclear, but it is believed to be a traditional Russian variety grown in home gardens for generations before reaching Western gardeners. The medium-sized (4–8 oz), flattened to slightly oblate, lemon-yellow to pale golden-orange fruits are distinguished from other yellow heirloom tomatoes by their flavor profile — Azoychka has a bright, tangy, citrusy complexity rarely found in yellow tomatoes, which typically run sweet and mild; the flavor has been compared to a lemon-spiked yellow tomato with good acid balance for a yellow-fruited type. The combination of mild sweetness and tanginess makes Azoychka outstanding for fresh slicing, tomato salads where both color and flavor stand out, and gazpacho. An indeterminate variety of moderate size and good productivity; excellent for any region with a long warm season.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Riesentraube (German for 'giant bunch of grapes') is a 19th-century German heirloom cherry tomato that produces the most spectacular, enormous hanging clusters of small red fruits of any heirloom variety — clusters of 20–40 or more fruits per stem that cascade down the vine in a grape-like arrangement of extraordinary visual impact.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; provide substantial staking, caging, or trellis support as plants grow vigorously to 6–8 feet; Riesentraube benefits from pruning to 1–2 leaders for best cluster development and air circulation. Riesentraube is a German heirloom cherry tomato documented in American seed catalogs by the 1850s and one of the oldest cherry tomato varieties maintained in continuous cultivation; the name 'Riesentraube' translates as 'giant bunch of grapes,' which perfectly describes the plant's most distinctive characteristic — enormous, pendulous clusters of 20, 30, or even 40+ small (1–1.5 oz) round, red fruits hanging in tight, cascade-like grape formations from the indeterminate vines. Each individual fruit has a pointed blossom end, which together with the cluster arrangement gives the plant an unmistakable profile compared to modern cherry tomatoes. The flavor is rich, tangy, and well-balanced with classic cherry tomato sweetness; the pointed fruits with thicker skin crack less than many cherry types and hold on the cluster longer after ripening. Riesentraube is both ornamentally spectacular in the garden (the cascading clusters are genuinely striking) and extremely productive, with harvests continuing from midsummer through first frost.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Garden Peach is a unique and charming heirloom tomato that genuinely resembles a peach — producing small, round, blush-yellow to pale cream fruits covered in a delicate fuzz of dense trichomes that gives them the texture and appearance of a ripe peach skin — one of the most visually distinctive and surprising novelty tomatoes available.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Garden Peach is an indeterminate, moderately sized variety requiring staking or caging; it is quite productive and continues bearing through the season. Garden Peach tomato is a remarkable heirloom curiosity that mimics a real peach in appearance with unusual fidelity — the small (2–3 oz), round fruits have pale cream to blush-yellow skin covered in tiny dense hairs (trichomes) that create a distinctly fuzzy, velvet-textured surface virtually identical to peach fuzz; when held in the hand, a ripe Garden Peach tomato is nearly indistinguishable from a small peach by touch alone. The flavor is sweet and mild with a hint of fruity peach character, very low acidity, and soft, juicy texture — appealing to those who find standard tomatoes too acidic. The novelty factor is considerable: Garden Peach tomatoes generate immediate curiosity and conversation, and children in particular are captivated by the peach resemblance; they are excellent for farm stands and markets as well as home garden curiosity. The fuzzy skin, unlike most tomatoes, does not split in rain or overhead irrigation. A productive and reliable variety for any garden where the growing season exceeds 70 days.
